coredata.dc:title: Band structures of hybrid graphene quantum dots with magnetic flux
coredata.dc:creator: Lemaalem, Bouchaib
Zahidi, Youness
Jellal, Ahmed
coredata.dc:description:
We study the band structures of hybrid graphene quantum dots subject to a magnetic flux and electrostatic potential. The system is consisting of a circular single layer graphene surrounded by an infinite bilayer graphene. By solving the Dirac equation we obtain the solution of the energy spectrum in two regions. For the valley K, it is found that the magnetic flux strongly acts by decreasing the gap and shifting energy levels away from zero radius with some oscillations, which are not observed for null flux case. As for the valley
K
′
, the energy levels rapidly increase when the radius increases. A number of oscillations appeared that is strongly dependent on the values taken by the magnetic flux.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hybrid graphene
Quantum dots
Magnetic flux
Electrostatic potential

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (Color online.) Circular SLG quantum dot in the presence of the magnetic flux Φ surrounded by an infinite BLGS.

(Color online.) Energy levels of the valley K as a function of the dot radius r 0 for m = 0,±1,±2,±3 in the presence of the biased potential U 1 = −U 2 = 0.1 eV and the magnetic flux Φ = ±
1
2
: (a) and (b), ±
3
2
: (c) and (d), ±
5
2
: (e) and (f). The solid (dashed) curves are for m > 0 (m < 0).

(Color online.) Energy levels of the valley K′ as a function of the dot radius r 0 for m = 0,±1,±2,±3 in the presence of the biased potential U 1 = −U 2 = 0.1 eV and the magnetic flux Φ = ±
1
2
: (a) and (b), ±
3
2
: (c) and (d), ±
5
2
: (e) and (f). The solid (dashed) curves are for m > 0 (m < 0).

head.ce:title: Band structures of hybrid graphene quantum dots with magnetic flux

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Theoretical Physics, Faculty of Sciences, Chouaïb Doukkali University, El Jadida, Morocco
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Theoretical Physics, Faculty of Sciences, Chouaïb Doukkali University, El Jadida, Morocco

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: EMAFI, Polydisciplinary Faculty, Sultan Moulay Selimane University, Khouribga, Morocco
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: EMAFI, Polydisciplinary Faculty, Sultan Moulay Selimane University, Khouribga, Morocco

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
We study the band structures of hybrid graphene quantum dots subject to a magnetic flux and electrostatic potential. The system is consisting of a circular single layer graphene surrounded by an infinite bilayer graphene. By solving the Dirac equation we obtain the solution of the energy spectrum in two regions. For the valley K, it is found that the magnetic flux strongly acts by decreasing the gap and shifting energy levels away from zero radius with some oscillations, which are not observed for null flux case. As for the valley
K
′
, the energy levels rapidly increase when the radius increases. A number of oscillations appeared that is strongly dependent on the values taken by the magnetic flux.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) in graphene are very small particles with unique electronic and optical properties [1–6]. Since they are highly tunable, then they can serve as interesting building blocks for materials that might be used to advance a wide range of applications such as solar cells, medical imaging and quantum computing. Because of the Klein tunneling effect and the absence of the gap in the energy spectrum, Dirac fermions cannot be confined by electrostatic potentials [7]. One solution to overcome such situation is to realize QDs for instance using thin single-layer graphene (SLG) strips [8,9] or nonuniform magnetic fields [10]. Generally, the electronic and optical properties of fermions in graphene depend on shapes and edges of QDS [11].

On the other hand, A-B bilayer graphene (BLG) consists of two SLG sheets where the A and B atoms in different layers are on top of each other [12], called also Bernal stacking. The most important interaction between the two layers is represented by a direct overlap integral between A and B atoms on top of each other. BLG presents some particularities for instance an external electric field, realized by external gate potentials, can induce a tunable band gap in its energy spectrum contrary to SLG.

Very recently a new generation of circular graphene QDs has been proposed based on a hybrid system [13]. Indeed, it was demonstrated that charge carriers can be confined in SLG and BLG islands in a hybrid QD-like structure made of SLG-BLG junctions. As a result, it is found that the energy levels exhibit characteristics of interface states in addition to the emergence of anti-crossings and closing of the band gap in the presence of a bias potential.

Motivated by the results reported in [13], we consider a geometry made of a circular SLG QD subjected to a magnetic flux and surrounded by an infinite BLG sheet as depicted in Fig. 1 . We solve Dirac equation in both regions and determine in the first stage the eigenspinors. To derive equations governing the energy levels, we use the zigzag boundary conditions at interface. We numerically analyze our results and show that the magnetic flux differently acts on the energy levels for both valleys K and
K
′
.

We consider a geometry made of a circular SLG QD of radius
r
0
in the presence of a magnetic flux ϕ embedded in infinite BLG containing
A
1
,
B
1
in first layer and
A
2
,
B
2
in second one (Fig. 1).

The Hamiltonian describing the motion of the charge carriers in monolayer graphene is given by
(1)
H =
v
F
σ
→
⋅ (
p
→
+ e
A
→
) +
U
1
I
where the vector potential associated to the magnetic flux Φ is given by [14]
(2)
A ( r ) =
h
e
Φ
2 π r
e
θ
ˆ
and
e
θ
ˆ
is the unit vector for the azimuthal,
U
1
is the potential applied to SLG,
σ
i
are Pauli matrices. To do not couple the two valleys K and
K
′
, we assume that the spatial extension of the flux line is large in comparison to the lattice constant.

To determine the eigenspinors of fermions in monolayer graphene subjected to magnetic flux, we consider the basis

in polar coordinates ( r , θ ) because of the system symmetry. The valley index τ = + 1 refers to K and τ = − 1 to

With the help of some relations between Bessel functions, we finally obtain the eigenspinors in monolayer graphene, which are

These results are different compared to the energy spectrum of a circular graphene quantum dot with subjected to a perpendicular magnetic field [15].

resulted from the Mexican-hat shaped low-energy dispersion in pristine BLG. In the limit Δ U ≪ t it behaves as potential scale, i.e. Δ ≈ Δ U .

As concerning the valley
K
′
, one can easy show that the radial parts of the corresponding eigenspinors can be linked to those for the valley K. Indeed, solving the eigenvalue equation with the basis
(26)

to end up with the relations

In the next we will see how the above results will be used to analyze the influence of the applied magnetic flux on the energy levels.

To derive equations describing the energy levels for the two valleys, we use the zigzag boundary conditions [17]. Then, at the interface ξ = 1 , namely r =
r
0
, between SLG and BLG we have the continuities
(31)
Ψ
A
τ
( 1 , θ ) =
Φ

( 1 , θ )

τ

Φ
B

( 1 , θ )

B

τ
( 1 , θ )

(34)

for the valley K ( τ = + 1 ) such that the matrix is

, we fix τ = − 1 and use the corresponding eigenspinors to end up with

with the parameters

c

N

and

−
are two constants of normalization. At this level we point out that the energy levels for both valleys are solutions of the two determinants
(40)
det ⁡
M
+
= 0 ,
det ⁡
M
−
= 0
It is clear that form the complexity of the special functions involved in the eigenspinors, it is not easy to analytically derive an explicit expression of the energy levels. Then, to investigate the basic features of our hybrid system we will proceed numerically.

In this section, we will investigate the energy levels E (eV) dependence on the dot radius for both valleys K and
K
′
. It is convenient for our task to choose the angular momenta m = 0 (black), ±1 (blue), ±2 (red) and ±3 (green), magnetic flux Φ = ±
1
2
, ±
3
2
, ±
5
2
and the biased potential
U
1
= −
U
2
= 0.1 eV. We emphasis that the solid black horizontal lines in all figures show the band gap.

The behavior of the energy levels E as a function of the dot radius
r
0
for the valley K is plotted in Fig. 2 with Φ = ±
1
2
((a) and (b)), ±
3
2
((c) and (d)), and ±
5
2
((e) and (f)). As a first result regarding K, we observe a difference in energy resulted from ±m because
E
K
( m ) ≠
E
K
( − m ) . Such behavior is similar to that observed for monolayer and bilayer quantum dot flakes in zero magnetic field [5,18]. Moreover, this result does not hold for the valley K levels in absence of the magnetic flux [13]. Then the difference in energy is a consequence of the applied magnetic flux in the central region.

Fig. 2(a) presents the behavior of the energy levels E for the value Φ =
1
2
. We notice that the energy levels are shifted and band gap decreased compared to the non-flux case [13]. As long as the radius increases we see that E increase but gap continue decreasing. We observe another behavior in Fig. 2(b) with Φ = −
1
2
. Indeed, the energy levels show a maximum corresponds to m = 0 , m = − 1 , m = − 2 and m = − 3 and
r
0
= 0 , after they oscillate to reach a minimum that decrease by increasing the radius
r
0
and then increase. Moreover, we observe the crossing between the levels of m, especially for weak dot radius.

In Fig. 2(c) we increase the magnetic flux by choosing Φ =
3
2
. In the this case, the shift of the energy levels becomes clear, especially for positive values of m and the decrease in gap as well. Remarkably for the negative value Φ = −
3
2
in Fig. 2(d) we notice that the values m = 0 ,
− 1 ,
− 2 show a similar behavior as in Fig. 2(b) but with less oscillation. Moreover, we observe that additional values of m have a similar behavior, especially for m = − 1 ,
− 2 . It is clearly seen that m = 1 ,
0 ,
− 1 ,
− 2 ,
− 3 correspond to a minima that decrease by increasing the dot radius, respectively.

By increasing the magnetic flux to ϕ =
5
2
in Fig. 2(e), we notice that the shift and gap become more important for positive values of the angular momenta m. As for ϕ = −
5
2
in Fig. 2(f), we observe that the number of oscillations increases to six corresponding to the quantum numbers m = 0 , m = ± 1 and m = ± 2 ,
3 . Here also m = 2 ,
1 ,
0 ,
− 1 ,
− 2 ,
− 3 correspond to a minima that decrease by increasing the dot radius, respectively.

Now, we switch to the case for the
K
′
valley. We note that like in the case of the K valley, we have
E
K
′
( m ) ≠
E
K
′
( − m ) , which is similar to that in the non-flux case [13]. The energy levels for the
K
′
valley are depicted in Fig. 3 for the magnetic flux Φ = ±
1
2
, ±
3
2
and ±
5
2
. We can clearly see from Fig. 3a, that the energy levels rapidly increase and the particularity is seen for m = − 1 , where the level overcomes that for m = 1 . The situation is completely changed for Φ = −
1
2
in Fig. 3(b) because we observe that the level m = − 1 is under m = 1 .

The behavior of the energy levels becomes more clear in Fig. 3(c) with Φ =
3
2
compared to Fig. 3(a). Now as concerning the negative value Φ = −
3
2
, Fig. 3(d) shows a mixing of the energy levels and we observe that some oscillations with different amplitudes start to take place. These are corresponding to the quantum numbers m = 0 (black solid line), m = 1 (blue solid line), m = 2 (red solid line), m = 3 (green solid line). We now consider Φ =
5
2
in Fig. 3(e) and observe an important change in the energy levels such that the shifts increase. In Fig. 3(f) with Φ = −
5
2
, we notice that the number of oscillations increase as compared to the case with Φ =
1
2
. These results show the manifestation of the magnetic flux and its impact on the energy levels.

We have studied the influence of a magnetic flux on the energy levels of a hybrid graphene. More precisely, we have considered a graphene quantum dot surrounded by a infinite bilayer graphene. Our result showed that the energy levels can oscillate, increase and shift under appropriate choice of flux and variation of the dot radius. We have found that the energy levels of the valleys K and
K
′
present different behaviors.
Bouchaib Lemaalem: Data curation, Investigation. Youness Zahidi: Data curation, Investigation. Ahmed Jellal: Data curation, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. V. Rozhkov, G. Giavaras, Y. P. Bliokh, V. Freilikher, and F. Nori, Phys. Rep. 503, 77 (2011).
Z. Z. Zhang, K. Chang, and F. M. Peeters, Phys. Rev. B 77, 235411 (2008).
D. Bischoff, A. Varlet, P. Simonet, M. Eich, H. C. Overweg, T. Ihn, and K. Ensslin, Appl. Phys. Rev. 2, 031301 (2015).
B. Trauzettel, D. V. Bulaev, D. Loss, and G. Burkard, Nat. Phys. 3, 192 (2007).
J. M. Pereira Jr, F. M. Peeters, P. Vasilopoulos, R. N. Costa Filho, and G. A. Farias, Phys. Rev. B 79, 195403 (2009).
P. Recher and B. Trauzettel, Nanotechnol. 21, 302001 (2010).
M. I. Katsnelson, K. S. Novoselov, and A. K. Geim, Nat. Phys. 2, 620 (2006).
N. M. R. Peres, A. H. Castro Neto, and F. Guinea, Phys. Rev. B 73, 241403 (2006).
P. G. Silvestrov and K. B. Efetov, Phys. Rev. Lett. 98, 016802 (2007).
Alev Devrim Güçlü, Pawel Potasz, Marek Korkusinski, and Pawel Hawrylak, Graphene Quantum Dots (Springer Berlin Heidelberg, 2014).
E. McCann, D. S. L. Abergel, and V. I. Falko, Eur. Phys. J. Spec. Top. 148, 91 (2007).
M. Mirzakhani, M. Zarenia, S. A. Ketabi, D. R. da Costa, and F. M. Peeters, Phys. Rev. B 93, 165410 (2016).
Julia Heinl, Martin Schneider, and Piet W. Brouwer, Phys. Rev. B 87, 245426 (2013).
S. Schnez, K. Ensslin, M. Sigrist, and T. Ihn, Phys. Rev. B 78, 195427 (2008).
E. McCann, D. S. L. Abergel, and V. I. Falko, Eur. Phys. J. Spec. Top. 148, 91 (2007).
M. Koshino, T. Nakanishi, and T. Ando, Phys. Rev. B 82, 205436 (2010).
M. Grujić, M. Zarenia, A. Chaves, M. Tadić, G. A. Farias, and F. M. Peeters, Phys. Rev. B 84, 205441 (2011).
